title_en: The Quran: The Uncreated Speech of Allah
content_en:
One of the central theological controversies of early Islamic history was the question of whether the Quran — the words of Allah — is created or uncreated. Al-Uthaymin explains this dispute in detail in his commentary, because Ibn Taymiyyah's al-Wasitiyyah devotes significant attention to it and because misunderstanding on this question has serious consequences for how a Muslim relates to the Quran.

The Mu'tazilah, under the intellectual leadership of scholars like al-Juba'i, argued that the Quran is created. Their reasoning was philosophical: Allah is eternal and unique; if the Quran were eternal and uncreated, it would imply a co-eternal partner with Allah, violating tawhid. The logical conclusion of this position is that the Quran is a created object in the same category as any other created thing — though of course a uniquely noble one.

Al-Uthaymin explains why Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal — who suffered imprisonment and flogging under the Mu'tazili-influenced caliph al-Ma'mun rather than affirm the createdness of the Quran — held this position with such conviction. The Ahl us-Sunnah position, which Ahmad defended with extraordinary courage, is that the Quran is the speech (kalam) of Allah — and Allah's speech is an attribute of Allah Himself. Attributes of Allah are not created. Therefore, the Quran, as the speech of Allah, is uncreated.

Al-Uthaymin carefully distinguishes what is meant by 'the Quran is uncreated' from what is not meant. The physical copies (masahif) — the ink, the paper — are created objects. The sounds produced by a reciter are created events. What is uncreated is the speech of Allah itself: the words, the meanings, the divine expression. This distinction was refined by later Ahl us-Sunnah scholars to address the confusion that arose from imprecise formulations.

Ibn Taymiyyah's position, as explained by al-Uthaymin, also addresses those who went to the opposite extreme and made confusing statements that seemed to imply that the letters and sounds of recitation are themselves attributes of Allah in an unqualified sense. Al-Uthaymin explains the correct formulation: the Quran — meaning the divine words and meanings — is the uncreated speech of Allah; the recitation and writing of those words are human acts and created in that sense. But the words being recited, which are the same words Allah spoke, are not created.

The practical importance of this question for the believer is significant. If the Quran were created, it would be in the same ontological category as other created things — elevated in rank, certainly, but finite, contingent, and potentially revisable (if Allah could 'speak' a new creation). The Ahl us-Sunnah position — that the Quran is the uncreated speech of Allah — establishes its absolute authority, its perfection, and the impossibility of any legislation superseding or supplementing it with divine authority. No human being, no institution, and no experience can claim to add to or modify the uncreated divine word.

Al-Uthaymin also discusses the eternity of Allah's attribute of speech (kalam). He explains the Ahl us-Sunnah position that Allah's attribute of speech is eternal — He speaks as He wills, when He wills — as opposed to the position that would make divine speech a single, unchanging, eternal entity (a position associated with some Ash'ari formulations). The relationship between the eternity of the attribute and the temporal expression of specific speech (such as the revealed Quran) is a matter the great scholars addressed, and al-Uthaymin guides the student through these carefully.

The chapter ends with the implications for how a Muslim approaches the Quran in worship: reciting, memorizing, and pondering the uncreated words of Allah is itself a profound act of worship — an act of meeting the divine communication in a way unlike any other experience available to human beings.
